Genotype QC to HRC ¦ eqtl_genotypes_hg38_preTM

DRAGON-Data Pipeline, Cardiff University

27-08-2025

Dataset pre-processing

The PLINK files provided contain 654027 genotyped markers on 145 individuals.

Original PLINK files are available here.

Non-standard chromosomes

Warning! 10 markers outside of standard (autosomal, X, Y, PAR, MTDNA) chromosomes were detected in the dataset. These have been excluded prior to any quality control steps.

Mitochondrial DNA

1138 SNPs were detected in the mitochondrial DNA. These have been separated into their own PLINK files and are available in the curated data folder.

QC Step 1: Missingness Rates

Samples

A large proportion of SNP assays failing on any single individual may be indicative of a poor quality DNA sample, which leads to aberrant genotype calling (Turner et al. 2011, PMID: 21234875). These samples should be removed from the dataset.

Optimal genotyping coverage was set at 95% (red line). 0 individuals were removed due to low coverage.

SNPs

Markers in which the genotyping has performed poorly can be identified by assessing their call rates over the whole dataset after discarding problematic samples (Turner et al. 2011, PMID: 21234875). SNPs with a low call rate are more likely to be affected by genotyping errors and should be excluded from further analyses.

Optimal per-marker call rate was set at 95% (red line). 8739 SNPs were removed due to low call rate.

QC Step 2: Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium (HWE)

Allele frequencies in random-mating populations usually follow the Hardy-Weinberg proportions, unless they are under strong selective pressures. In the quality control of array datasets for GWAS, errors in genotyping tend to create deviations from HWE, as they result in a greater number of heterozygote calls (Graffelman 2017, PMID: 28374190).

The genome-wide significant p-value threshold for the Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium exact test was set at 1.00e-06. 75 SNPs were removed due to deviations from HWE (red diamonds). The solid blue line indicates the expectation for the relationship between pairs of genotype frequencies according to the Hardy-Weinberg principles.

HWE testing was performed by calculating a “mid” p-value instead of an exact p-value (Graffelman & Moreno 2013, PMID: 23934608).

QC Step 3: Sex chromosome checks

This test assesses homozygosity estimates from the X chromosome using the inbreeding coefficient F (Wright 1921, PMID: 17245962) calculated for each sample using LD-independent X-linked markers (\(r^{2} \le 0.2\)). Samples at the extremes of the distribution of the F statistic are likely genetic females (low F) or genetic males (high F). Coverage for Y-linked markers can also be inspected to more accurately infer the sex of ambiguous samples. Furthermore, the combination of both metrics can pinpoint individuals as potential carriers of sex chromosome aneuploidies such as the Turner (X0; high F and low Y coverage) or Klinefelter (XXY; low F and high Y coverage) syndromes (Igo et al. 2016, PMID: 27367164).

Homozygosity thresholds (red lines) set at 0.2 for genetic females and 0.8 for genetic males. 0 individuals were seen between both values. 0 individuals were identified as potentially having an abnormal sex chromosome karyotype (orange diamonds), and could benefit from addional quality control if raw genotype intensity data is available.

In some cases, Y chromosome coverage rates of female samples can be >10%, though there should still be a clear difference between female and male samples in this metric. This is an issue reported for some legacy genotyping arrays where autosomal or X-linked probes were erroneously coded as Y-linked.

QC Step 4: Population structure and in-sample relatedness

PC-AiR (Conomos et al. 2015, PMID: 25810074) was performed using the implementation in the GENESIS software suite. This is a modified version of the Principal Components Analysis (PCA) approach for the detection of population structure in genome-wide data. Unlike the standard method, PC-AiR may be used if some individuals in the sample are closely related, as it generates principal components that are robust to family structure.

Results from a PC-AiR analysis on 98780 LD-independent markers outside of genomic regions with long-range LD. Red lines indicate the area delimited by the mean +/- 1.96 standard deviations of the first two principal components.

The output of PC-AiR can be used by PC-Relate (Conomos et al. 2016, PMID: 26748516), an approach to de-confound estimates of genetic kinship from population structure. The PC-Relate coefficient \(\Phi\) can be interpreted in the same way as the PLINK metric \(\hat\pi{}\) to identify groups of related individuals (Manichaikul et al. 2010, PMID: 20926424).

For each pair of samples in the dataset, the plot shows proportions of markers with no identical-by-descent (IBD) alleles and estimated kinship values. These have been corrected using 5 principal components and rescaled to the equivalent PLINK metric. 6 pairs of individuals have a relatedness coefficient greater than 0.09375 (red line). Among those, 0 pairs have been identified as potentially duplicated samples or monozygotic twins.

QC Step 5: Biogeographic ancestry inference

These analyses are based on the procedure described in Legge et al. 2019 (PMID: 30647433) and updated in Smart et al. 2024 (in prep). The biogeographic ancestries inferred are based on the standardised system by Huddart et al. 2019 (PMID: 30506572).

2490 unrelated individuals from the 1000 Genomes Phase 3 (Auton et al. 2015; PMID: 26432245) were used to infer Ancestry Informative Markers (AIMs). These were defined as LD-independent variants overlapping the input dataset, outside of genomic regions with long-range LD (Price et al. 2008; PMID: 18606306), and with a Wright’s FST value above the 97.5th percentile in at least one pairwise population comparison. The input and reference datasets were then trimmed to contain only AIMs and merged. Only 6 (out of 9) ancestries are available in this reference dataset.

Results from a PC-AiR analysis on 25176 AIMs. 100 principal components were computed in the reference samples. Using the “prediction” algorithm implemented in PC-AiR, the input dataset samples (grey squares) were projected into the reference PC-coordinates.

The Tracy-Widom test (Patterson et al. 2006; PMID: 17194218) selected the 31 first eigenvectors of the dataset for the ancestry inference procedure. A model based on Fisher’s Linear Discriminant Analysis (Yang et al. 2005; PMID: 16193326) was trained on the known ancestries of the reference samples with principal components as predictors.

LDA model training statistics, based on 5 repeats of a 10-foldcross-validation routine.

Biogeographic Ancestry Prevalence Sensitivity Specificity Balanced Accuracy
African American / Afro-Caribbean 5.25% 97.71% 99.06% 98.38%
East Asian 20.24% 100.00% 100.00% 100.00%
European 20.63% 97.90% 100.00% 98.95%
Latino 13.46% 99.40% 99.36% 99.38%
Central / South Asian 19.44% 100.00% 100.00% 100.00%
Sub-Saharan African 20.97% 96.13% 100.00% 98.07%

The trained LDA classification model was then applied to the principal components of the input dataset to predict the most likely ancestral category of each sample. A threshold for ancestry inference was set by finding, for each ancestral category, the probability cutoff maximising the value of Youden’s J index (Perkins & Schisterman 2006; PMID: 16410346). To avoid assigning any individual to multiple ancestries, only probability cutoffs greater than 0.501 were evaluated.

LDA model results.

Predicted Biogeographic Ancestry Probability Threshold Number of Samples
Unclassified NA 2
African American / Afro-Caribbean 0.501 9
East Asian 0.501 1
European 0.997 125
Latino 0.501 3
Central / South Asian 0.501 3
Sub-Saharan African 0.886 2

For 2 individuals none of the ancestral probabilities reach their optimal probability threshold. These individuals have been labelled “Unclassified”. Detailed inspection of ancestral probabilities is recommended to evaluate the potential admixture patterns of unclassified individuals.

Additional Operations

LiftOver

The curated genotype data in build GRCh37 was re-mapped to build GRCh38. The positions of 645046 markers were successfully converted using the LiftOver procedure implemented in the R package rtracklayer. Any markers in conversion-unstable loci (Ormond et al. 2021, PMID: 33822888) were identified and removed to prevent downstream errors.

Whenever possible, converted markers were renamed to match dbSNP release 156 using the 1000 Genomes Phase 3 (2022) files from the PLINK2 website. 523874 markers were assigned dbSNP IDs. The IDs of other markers were set to CHR:BP:REF:ALT.

Note this LiftOver procedure is designed for bi-allelic SNP content as expected in array datasets. See Genovese et al. 2024 (PMID: 33822888) for a more general alternative.

Harmonisation to imputation reference

Genotype Harmonizer was used to align curated markers to the Trans-Omics for Precision Medicine (TopMED) imputation panel. 523575 markers were successfully harmonised. Per-chromosome block-gzipped VCFs have been created for submission to the TopMED Imputation Server.

Output file location

645203 markers and 145 individuals passed all quality control checks.

Curated genotype files.

Reference and merged files used for ancestry inference.

Re-mapped files generated by LiftOver.

Imputation-ready files generated by Genotype Harmonizer.

Parent folder including all intermediate genotype files.

NOTE: Curated files are provided in PLINK2 .pgen/.psam/.pvar format, but are identical in marker and sample content to intermediate .qc2 files in PLINK1 .bed/.bim/.fam format.
